summary: Independent events multiply, mutually exclusive events add, and "without replacement" changes the probabilities as items are removed.

formulas.name: General "or" rule
formulas.expression: P(A \text{ or } B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \text{ and } B)

explanation: Two events are independent if one doesn't affect the other's probability (like separate coin flips); their "and" probability is the product. Events are mutually exclusive if they can't both happen (like rolling a 2 and a 5 on one die); their "or" probability is just the sum. If events can overlap, subtract the overlap: \(P(A\text{ or }B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A\text{ and }B)\). "Without replacement" problems (like drawing cards without putting them back) are dependent: each draw changes the pool, so recompute the probability at each step using the remaining items. "At least one" problems are often easiest to solve using the complement: \(P(\text{at least one}) = 1 - P(\text{none})\).
